title: Bit Manipulation
description: Working directly on a number's binary representation using bitwise operators — fast, constant-space tricks for a whole class of problems.
objectives: Review the core bitwise operators: AND, OR, XOR, NOT, left shift, right shift
Check, set, clear, and toggle a specific bit
Use XOR's self-cancelling property to solve the Single Number problem
Count set bits and check if a number is a power of two

sections.text: 1. Recap: Operators, Revisited at the Bit Level

Operators covered comparison and arithmetic on whole values. Bitwise operators work one level deeper — directly on the individual binary digits (bits) that make up an int (recall Type Modifiers and how int is stored).

rows: shifts bits left, filling with 0s (multiplies by 2 per shift)
shifts bits right (divides by 2 per shift, for non-negative values)

sections.text: 3. Bit Tricks: Check, Set, Clear, Toggle

sections.code:
int n = 10; // binary: 1010
int i = 1;  // the bit position we care about (0-indexed from the right)

bool isSet   = (n >> i) & 1;   // CHECK bit i: shift it to position 0, then isolate it
int  setBit  = n | (1 << i);   // SET bit i to 1
int  clearBit = n & ~(1 << i); // CLEAR bit i to 0
int  toggleBit = n ^ (1 << i); // TOGGLE bit i (1 -> 0, or 0 -> 1)

sections.text: 1 << i produces a number that is all 0s except a single 1 at position i — this is the standard building block for every bit trick above. Recognizing this pattern (1 << i) is the key to reading and writing bit manipulation code.
For any value x: x ^ x = 0 (a number XORed with itself is always 0), and x ^ 0 = x (XOR with 0 changes nothing). XOR is also commutative and associative, so the order of operations doesn't matter.
XOR-ing every number in the array together makes every PAIRED number cancel itself out to 0 (x ^ x = 0), leaving only the unpaired number standing — because anything XORed with 0 is unchanged.

sections.title: Tracing the XOR chain
points: Final result: 4 — the single unpaired number

sections.text: n & (n - 1) clears the lowest set bit of n. For a power of two (binary form 1000...0), clearing that single bit leaves exactly 0 — for any other number, it doesn't, which is why this check works.

sections.title: A row of light switches, toggled and paired off
sections.text: Imagine a row of light switches where every switch gets flipped exactly twice except one, which gets flipped once — after all the flipping, every twice-flipped switch is back to its original OFF state, and only the once-flipped switch is left ON. That's precisely what XOR-ing every array value together does: paired values cancel back to 0, leaving only the unpaired one visible.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing & (bitwise AND) with && (logical AND) — & operates on individual bits, && operates on whole boolean conditions.
Forgetting parentheses around shift expressions — (1 << i) needs its own parentheses when combined with |, &, or ^ due to operator precedence rules.
Assuming >> always behaves like dividing by 2 for negative numbers — right shift on signed negative values is implementation-defined/arithmetic-shift behavior, not simple division.
Reaching for a hash set to solve Single Number before recognizing the O(1)-space XOR trick — the paired-cancellation pattern is what makes this solvable without extra memory.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What two properties of XOR make the Single Number trick work?
questions.answer: x ^ x = 0 (a number cancels with itself) and x ^ 0 = x (XOR with 0 leaves a value unchanged) — so XOR-ing every element together cancels all paired values, leaving only the unpaired one.

questions.question: How do you check whether bit i of a number n is set?
questions.answer: (n >> i) & 1 — shift bit i down to position 0, then mask with 1 to isolate it.

questions.question: How can n & (n - 1) be used to check if n is a power of two?
questions.answer: n & (n - 1) clears the lowest set bit; a power of two has exactly one set bit, so clearing it leaves 0 — checking n > 0 && (n & (n-1)) == 0 confirms this.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Bit manipulation works directly on a number's binary representation using &, |, ^, ~, <<, and >>. The 1 << i pattern isolates a single bit at position i, enabling check/set/clear/toggle operations on any specific bit. XOR has a uniquely useful property for problems like Single Number: x ^ x = 0 and x ^ 0 = x, so XOR-ing an entire array together cancels every value that appears in pairs, leaving only the value that appears once — solved in O(n) time and O(1) extra space, no hash set required. The same bit-level thinking powers other tricks too, like n & (n-1) clearing the lowest set bit to test for powers of two.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Bit Manipulation
sections.text: 1 << i = a single 1 at spot i. OR to set, AND-with-NOT to clear, XOR to toggle. XOR cancels pairs to 0 — pair up and vanish, the odd one stays.

sections.title: Single Number
sections.problemStatement: Given a non-empty array where every element appears exactly twice except for one, find that single element. Must run in O(n) time with O(1) extra space.
hints: This is the lesson's own worked example — try implementing it yourself before checking back against the XOR explanation above.
XOR every element together in a single pass: x ^ x = 0 cancels every paired value, and x ^ 0 = x leaves the unpaired one untouched.
The O(1) space requirement rules out a hash set (which would be O(n) space) — XOR is exactly the trick that satisfies both constraints simultaneously.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Hash map counting occurrences, then scan for the one with count 1 — works but violates the O(1) space constraint.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: XOR every element together — pairs cancel to 0, leaving only the unpaired value.
